pattern: Reversing the digits of an integer through its decimal-string form (and letting any leading zero simply fall away) is enough to answer the whole question with one subtraction and an absolute value.
hints: Convert the number to its digit string, reverse that string, and convert it back to a number.
A trailing zero in the original number becomes a leading zero in the reversed digits, which simply disappears once it is turned back into a number.
The answer is just the absolute value of the original number minus this reversed number -- a palindrome-like number naturally gives zero.

edge_inputs.description: statement example two, a trailing zero disappears on reversal
statement example three, a single-digit palindrome
several trailing zeros collapse away on reversal
a value one below the largest power of ten in range

stress.description: random integers across the full allowed range
